verdict_reasoning: ATP5F1B (ATP synthase F1 beta subunit) is canonically a mitochondrial matrix-facing protein that forms the catalytic F1 head of complex V on the inner mitochondrial membrane. However, there is well-documented literature showing that intact F1-ATP synthase (including the beta subunit) is displayed on the outer plasma membrane surface of tumor cells and certain normal cell types (endothelial cells, hepatocytes), where it functions as a cell-surface ATPase and receptor for ligands such as apolipoprotein A-I and angiostatin. This cancer/cell-state ecto-display qualifies as cell_state_induced contextual surface accessibility. The alias HEL-S-271 (human epididymis-specific expression) further hints at tissue-restricted display. Surface-directed therapeutic programs (e.g., antibody targeting of cell-surface F1-ATPase on tumor vasculature) have been described. Thus, while the dominant localization is mitochondrial, a documented non-baseline ecto-pool on cancer/endothelial cells establishes contextual surface accessibility.
predicted_key_uncertainty: Extent and wash-resistance of the ecto-ATP synthase pool on tumor/endothelial surfaces is not fully characterized; some studies dispute constitutive vs. inducible display.
